sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 24, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has three regions: (1) top-left shows a target pattern with white block above purple block, (2) top-right has two selector buttons (white and purple) with an indicator bar, (3) main area has a purple block above a white block (inverted from target). The goal appears to be matching the target pattern.
- Plan: Let me check if arrow keys move a cursor somewhere on the board, or if there's some other mechanic I'm missing. Let me also try pressing SPACE in the current state (white selected, unframed split).
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 23 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
MAJOR DISCOVERY! The RIGHT arrow key changed the board dramatically!
Before RIGHT:
- Framed block: white (id=12, 84 pixels, at [[25, 26], [25, 37], [31, 37], [31, 26]])
- Unframed block: merged white 10×10 (id=13, 100 pixels, at [[34, 27], [34, 36], [43, 36], [43, 27]])
- Gray frame: id=11, 30 pixels, at [[24, 25], [24, 38], [32, 38], ...]
After RIGHT:
- The gray frame moved/changed shape: now 43 pixels, boundary [[21, 39], [31, 49], [37, 43], ...] - this looks like a diamond/rhombus shape!
- The framed block changed: now 85 pixels, boundary [[23, 39], [31, 47], [35, 43], ...] - also a diamond shape!
- The white block below is still there but seems to have moved
Wait, looking at the image, the board now shows:
- A diamond/rhombus shape made of white and gray (the framed block became a diamond)
- A white square below it
So RIGHT arrow key ROTATES the framed block! It transformed from a rectangle to a diamond. This is a shape transformation mechanic!
Let me understand:
- The framed block was a white rectangle (6×14 or similar)
- After RIGHT, it became a white diamond/rhombus
- The gray frame also rotated to match
So the arrow keys rotate the shape! Let me try more rotations to see all possible shapes.
Also, SPACE with white selected + split block merged the unframed block to white 10×10. So SPACE with the same color as the block merges it.
Let me explore the rotation mechanic more. I should try LEFT, UP, DOWN to see what other transformations happen.
Actually wait - looking more carefully at the image, the framed block seems to have rotated into a diamond shape, and there's still a white square below. The unframed block seems to have stayed as a white square (merged).
Let me try more rotations to understand the full set of transformations.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: MAJOR DISCOVERY! The RIGHT arrow key ROTATES the framed block shape! It transformed from a rectangle to a diamond/rhombus. The arrow keys control shape transformations, not just timer navigation. Also, SPACE with white selected + split block merged the unframed block to white 10×10.
Plan: Explore the rotation mechanic by trying more arrow keys to see all possible shapes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state after RIGHT: framed block is a white diamond, unframed is white square
# Let me try more rotations to understand the shape cycle

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== Current state ===")
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
print("\n=== After RIGHT #2 ===")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
print("\n=== After RIGHT #3 ===")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
print("\n=== After RIGHT #4 ===")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 20 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
